Q1: I tried to keep things pretty consistent, honestly. With 20 courses to juggle, I had to be organized or I'd drown. I'd usually carve out time in the mornings before work and some evenings, maybe around 18 hours total each week. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd listen to lectures while doing chores or commuting. It wasn't perfect, but I had a system that worked for me.
Q2: I definitely aimed for regular times because that's what keeps me sane. Mornings were my anchor—I'd listen to lecture recordings with my coffee before the day got chaotic. But honestly, when deadlines piled up or life got busy, that routine would shift. I tried to stay consistent, but at 28 with limited resources, sometimes you just have to adapt to what the week throws at you.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check—like discussion boards or to see if new materials were posted. Sometimes it was just habit, like checking email. I'd also log in when I wanted to participate in discussions because I actually enjoyed connecting with other students. It wasn't always about assignments; sometimes it was just to stay in the loop.
Q4: Yes, definitely. After a few weeks, logging in became almost automatic, like brushing my teeth. I'd check in during my morning routine, scroll through discussions, see what videos were new. The mindfulness practice I started helped me be more intentional about it too—I'd take a breath, log in, and focus for a bit. It just became part of how I started my day.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I watched over 270 minutes of content because hearing explanations really helps things click. The discussion boards were also valuable—I posted quite a bit and interacted with peers because talking through ideas helps me understand them better. Those two resources were my lifeline.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete many assignments—only about 4% of them. It wasn't that I ignored them on purpose, but with limited resources and time constraints, I had to prioritize. Some readings I'd skim or skip if I could get the main points from the video lectures instead. I focused on what I knew would help me pass rather than trying to do everything perfectly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand the material or if it connected me with other students. Discussions were great because I could hear different perspectives and clarify my thinking. If something felt like busywork or didn't add to my understanding, I'd deprioritize it. I'm practical—I need to see the value in what I'm doing.
Q8: Oh, absolutely they felt different. Required activities had this weight to them, even if I didn't always complete them. Optional ones I'd only do if I genuinely found them interesting or if I had extra time, which was rare. The optional discussions I actually enjoyed because there was no pressure—I could engage authentically without worrying about grades.
Q9: All the time! I'd download lecture audio and listen offline constantly—on the bus, while cooking, during walks. I don't have the best internet access, so downloading materials once and studying them repeatedly was essential. I also took handwritten notes while listening, which the LMS would never capture. A lot of my real studying happened away from the screen.
Q10: Most of my studying, honestly. I'd listen to a lecture once online, then replay my downloaded version three or four more times while doing other things. I'd also talk through concepts with a friend who's in a similar program—we'd quiz each other over coffee. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but it was how I actually learned the material.
Q11: Constantly. With my internet situation, I couldn't afford to stream things multiple times. I'd access a video once, download it or take detailed notes, and then work with those materials for days. The LMS would show one view, but I might have engaged with that content a dozen times offline. It was just more practical and reliable for me.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when multiple courses had overlapping demands or when money was tight and I was stressed about other things. My attendance dropped to around 38% because sometimes life just got in the way. But the mindfulness practice helped me stay grounded—I'd acknowledge the stress and then refocus. My resilience isn't great, so I had to be gentle with myself.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed with responsibilities, I'd do the minimum on the LMS—maybe just check discussions or skim materials. I couldn't always give my full attention. Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor; being 28 with limited resources means I'm juggling a lot more than just coursework. The LMS engagement would dip when life demanded more.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd open materials but my mind would be elsewhere—worried about bills or tired from a long day. I'd scroll through a reading without really absorbing it, or have a video playing while I was mentally planning my week. Those moments happened, but I tried to catch myself and come back when I could actually focus. The mindfulness helped me notice when I was just going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments quite a bit, which is why my completion rate was so low. It wasn't that I didn't know they were important—I did. But with limited time and energy, I had to make choices. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later often didn't come. I focused on what I knew would help me pass the exams instead, which worked out okay in the end.
Q16: Deadlines definitely created spikes in my activity. I'd be more likely to log in, check requirements, and engage with materials when something was due soon. But I also tried not to let deadlines completely control me—I'd prioritize based on what was realistic. Sometimes I'd accept that I couldn't meet every deadline and focus on what mattered most for my grade.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback on my assignment work—I scored really well on what I did submit—it motivated me to keep going. Knowing I could do well when I put in the effort was encouraging. But feedback was also rare since I didn't submit much. The peer interactions in discussions felt like informal feedback too, and those kept me engaged.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could adapt it to my schedule and learning style. Bad because without strict requirements, I could let things slide. Having 20 courses meant I had to be selective about where I put my energy. The structure allowed me to focus on exams and discussions rather than every single assignment.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement so much easier for me—essential, really. The discussion boards were well-designed and felt welcoming. What made it harder was when materials required constant internet access or when there were too many small assignments that felt like busywork. I thrived when I could engage deeply with fewer, more meaningful activities.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out—exploring materials, reading discussions, watching videos—but struggling to actually produce work. Maybe they're overwhelmed or confused about expectations. Or maybe, like me, they're learning a lot through engagement but prioritizing differently when it comes to submissions. Activity doesn't always equal output.
Q21: That actually sounds like me! My LMS activity was moderate, but I performed reasonably well on exams because so much of my learning happened offline. I'd listen to lectures repeatedly, discuss concepts with friends, and really internalize the material away from the platform. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the course content.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you're on a page. That's useful for seeing general trends. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the conversations with peers outside the platform, and the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They capture behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and connecting with the learning community. It's not just about completing tasks—it's about being mentally present, curious, and invested in growing. For me, engagement looked like listening to lectures multiple times, participating in discussions, and actually thinking about how concepts applied to my life. It's an internal state, not just external activity.
Q24: So much wouldn't show. My offline listening sessions, my conversations with friends about course concepts, the way I'd think through ideas during my commute, my mindfulness practice that helped me stay focused and positive despite challenges. The data would show moderate engagement, but it wouldn't capture how much I actually learned or how meaningful this experience was for me despite my circumstances.
